steps: Represent a rotation by $(\mathbf{u}, \theta)$ with $\mathbf{u} \in S^2$ the axis and $\theta \in [0,\pi]$ the angle. The identity corresponds to any $(\mathbf{u}, 0)$; a $\pi$-rotation about $\mathbf{u}$ equals that about $-\mathbf{u}$.
This gives a homeomorphism $SO(3) \cong D^3 / (x \sim -x \text{ on } \partial D^3) = \mathbb{R}P^3$.
The $\mathbb{Z}/2$ in $H_1$ captures the fact that $\pi_1(SO(3)) = \mathbb{Z}/2$: a $2\pi$ rotation is contractible but a $4\pi$ rotation is not.
The double cover $SU(2) \cong S^3 \to SO(3)$ confirms this: $S^3$ is simply connected and covers $SO(3)$ with fibre $\mathbb{Z}/2$.
